disease.name: Intrahepatic Cholestasis of Pregnancy (ICP)
disease.note: Pregnancy-specific cholestatic liver disorder characterized by pruritus and elevated serum bile acids. Usually presents in late second or third trimester. Resolves spontaneously after delivery. Maternal prognosis is excellent but fetal risks include preterm labor, meconium-stained amniotic fluid, and sudden intrauterine fetal death (especially when bile acids >40 umol/L).

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Bile acids >40 (severe) -> ACTIVE management, delivery at 36-37w; Bile acids <40 (mild) -> MEDICAL management, IOL at 38-39w

presentingComplaints: Itching worse at night, especially palms and soles

historyFeatures.note: Cardinal symptom; intense; worst on palms and soles; nocturnal
No primary skin lesions; excoriations from scratching
Increased risk in twin and triplet pregnancies

laboratory.name: Fasting serum bile acids (total bile acids)
laboratory.indication: diagnostic and prognostic; >10 umol/L confirms ICP; >40 defines severe disease with highest fetal risk

laboratory.name: ALT and AST
laboratory.indication: elevated in 60% (usually 2-5x ULN); may be normal in mild disease

laboratory.name: ALP and GGT
laboratory.indication: ALP normally elevated in pregnancy; GGT elevated in 30%

laboratory.name: Viral hepatitis serology (HAV, HBV, HCV, HEV)
laboratory.indication: rule out viral hepatitis as cause of pruritus and LFT elevation

laboratory.name: Abdominal ultrasound
laboratory.indication: rule out gallstones/biliary obstruction; normal in ICP

imaging.name: Obstetric ultrasound
imaging.indication: fetal growth, amniotic fluid volume, biophysical profile

imaging.name: Fetal monitoring (NST/BPP)
imaging.indication: weekly or twice weekly if bile acids >40 from 34 weeks; no proven predictive value for IUFD

management.protocolReference: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Obstetric Cholestasis; SMFM Consult Series: ICP; ACOG Practice Bulletin

pathways.criteria: Pruritus + fasting bile acids 10-39 umol/L; normal or mildly elevated LFTs; no jaundice
pathways.treatment: First-line: ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) 10-15mg/kg/day PO (typically 500mg BID or 300mg TID). UDCA improves pruritus and LFTs but does not reduce fetal risks in meta-analyses. Antihistamines (chlorpheniramine 4mg PO TID) for pruritus. Emollients. Monitor symptoms weekly. Repeat bile acids and LFTs weekly. Antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 12mg IM q24h x 2) if planned delivery before 36w. Delivery: induction of labor at 38-39 weeks. Fetal monitoring: weekly NST/BPP from diagnosis.

pathways.criteria: Bile acids >=40 umol/L (highest fetal risk); or persistent jaundice; or previous adverse outcome
pathways.treatment: UDCA as above. Active fetal monitoring: twice weekly NST/BPP from diagnosis. Antenatal steroids if <36w. Plan delivery at 36-37 weeks (RCOG recommends 35-36w for bile acids >100). Meconium-stained liquor is common; be prepared for neonatal resuscitation. Intrapartum: continuous fetal monitoring. Neonatal pediatrics aware. Postpartum: ICP resolves within days of delivery. Repeat LFTs/bile acids at 6-8 weeks postpartum to confirm normalization (if not normalize, consider underlying cholestatic liver disease).

Add rifampicin 150-300mg PO BID (caution: hepatotoxic, monitor LFTs). Consider S-adenosyl-L-methionine 500mg PO BID (limited evidence). Cholestyramine 4g PO TID (poorly tolerated; binds bile acids in gut; note: may interfere with vitamin K absorption, monitor INR). Topical emollients with menthol 1% and aqueous cream. Consider early delivery if symptoms severe and gestational age >36 weeks.
